2005-11-01.title: Northern Ireland Paramilitary Group Disbands
2005-11-01.abstract: Irish Protestant paramilitary organization Loyalist Volunteer Force disbands; action is direct response to recent decision of Irish Republican Army to end armed campaign against Britain and destroy its weapons (S)

2005-11-01.title: Israeli Airstrike Kills 2 Palestinians
2005-11-01.abstract: An Israeli airstrike near Gaza City today killed two wanted Palestinians, including one that Israel had singled out as a top priority.

2005-11-01.title: Iraq holds lottery to set order of parties on Dec. 15 ballot
Bush announces $7.1 billion bird flu plan
Bomb kills 20 in Basra while U.S. toll spikes

2005-11-02.title: Kyrgyzstan Quiets Two Prisons; Troops Kill 4 Inmates in Battles
2005-11-02.abstract: Special forces soldiers stormed two prisons in this Central Asian nation on Tuesday, restoring at least the appearance of order to a federal penitentiary system that had partly slipped from government control last month. Four inmates were killed, the authorities said.     Two inmates died in a clash between government troops and prisoners loyal to a crime boss who the authorities said had operated with near impunity here inside Prison No. 31. The crime boss, Aziz Batukayev, was ultimately captured, the authorities said, and moved to the headquarters of Kyrgyzstan's successor to the Soviet K.G.B.

2005-11-02.title: Colombia Government Threatens Death Squad
2005-11-02.abstract: Colombian government warns of military strikes against United Self-Defense Forces of Colombia, country's largest death squad; warning comes day after third high official of Colombian secret police is forced to resign amid disclosures that secret police were preparing to sell intelligence to right-wing paramilitaries (S)

2005-11-02.title: Rural migrants to get more rights in China
Letter from New York: Battle against bird flu must start at its source
Blair ally quits cabinet a 2nd time amid scandal

2005-11-03.title: Briefly: Polled responses cool to Schwarzenegger
Libby to make first court appearance since indictment
Names of the Dead
2005-11-03.abstract: Defense Dept confirms deaths of five service members in Iraq (S)

2005-11-03.title: Briefly: 5 detainees released from Guantánamo Bay
World Briefing | Asia: Kashmir: Suicide Car Bomb Kills 5
2005-11-03.abstract: Suicide bombing kills five people in India-controlled area of Kashmir; Jaish-e-Muhammad, Pakistan-based militant group, takes credit for attack (S)

2005-11-04.title: Most Spaniards opposed to more Catalonia autonomy
Cardinal urges Catholics to heed modern science
EU looking into report of secret CIA jails
EU accepts denials of 2 members on CIA prisons

2005-11-05.title: U.S. Should Repay Millions to Iraq, a U.N. Audit Finds
2005-11-05.abstract: An auditing board sponsored by the United Nations recommended yesterday that the United States repay as much as $208 million to the Iraqi government for contracting work in 2003 and 2004 assigned to Kellogg, Brown & Root, the Halliburton subsidiary.     The work was paid for with Iraqi oil proceeds, but the board said it was either carried out at inflated prices or done poorly. The board did not, however, give examples of poor work.

2005-11-05.title: Spain's African Enclaves Are Migrants' Portals to Europe
2005-11-05.abstract: Zakaria Chouaib peered from his rocky perch on Mount Gourougou toward a stone and stucco settlement below. He had traveled overland about 2,000 miles from Ghana, largely on foot, to arrive within sight of his goal: an eightsquare-mile patch of Spanish territory on Africa's Mediterranean coast called Melilla.     Mr. Chouaib, 31, knew that if he could make it across the two razor-wire-topped cyclone fences separating Morocco from the enclave, he stood a good chance of being sent on to Spain and then disappearing into an increasingly borderless Europe.

2005-11-05.title: Immigrant Rioting Flares in France for Ninth Night
2005-11-05.abstract: France's worst urban violence in a decade exploded for a ninth night on Friday as bands of youths roamed the immigrant-heavy, working-class suburbs of Paris, setting fire to dozens of cars and buildings while the government struggled over the violence and the underlying frustrations fueling it.     The unrest, which has also spread to other parts of France with large North African and Arab populations, prompted the American and Russian governments to warn citizens visiting Paris to avoid its poor, outlying neighborhoods. France reduced train service to Charles de Gaulle Airport after two trains became targets of rioters earlier in the week.

2005-11-05.title: Iraq's Lethal Traffic: Warning! Anarchy Ahead
2005-11-05.abstract: It was Monday morning in one of the world's most dangerous cities, and already the traffic was causing a peculiar, decidedly nonmechanical sort of breakdown.     ''We are suffering psychologically from this,'' said Bessam Abdel Khalid, a 24-year-old minivan driver, staring dully through the windshield at the crush of cars idling in a frustrated mass near Baghdad University.

2005-11-05.title: Hemisphere Summit Marred by Violent Anti-Bush Protests
2005-11-05.abstract: President Bush's troubles trailed him to an international summit meeting here on Friday as anti-Bush protesters turned violent just blocks from the gathering site, and Hugo Chávez, Venezuela's fiery populist leader, rallied a soccer stadium filled with at least 25,000 people against the United States.     Mr. Chávez, who has tried to use the summit meeting to stage a showdown with Mr. Bush, pronounced dead a free trade accord backed by Mr. Bush, the Free Trade Area of the Americas. Left-wing groups throughout Latin America have long opposed the agreement, and some governments want more generous terms from Washington, so Mr. Bush had come here with hopes of jump-starting the stalled negotiations.

2005-11-06.title: West Bank town is proving ground for Hamas
Bush keeps low profile in the CIA leak case
Iran tries to burnish its image
Chief Syrian suspect is at center of power

2005-11-07.title: A New Sunni Party Raises Hopes of More Political Participation
2005-11-07.abstract: A group of Iraqi tribal sheiks have formed a political party aimed at capturing votes in a national election in December, one of the first signs that Sunni Arabs outside mainstream parties are moving into grass-roots politics.     A large group of tribal leaders, academics and other professionals met Saturday in Ramadi, the capital of Anbar Province, the only Iraqi province in which the population is almost entirely Sunni Arab, for a campaign kickoff by the new group, the National Public Democratic Movement, its leaders said Sunday.

2005-11-07.title: India and Pakistan open Kashmir border
White House Letter; Far Away From Home, No Rest for a Weary President
2005-11-07.abstract: White House Letter: Pres Bush's messy four-day trip to Latin America includes violent anti-Bush riots in Argentina and anti-American rally led by Pres Hugo Chavez of Venezuela; polls indicate that Bush is most unpopular American president ever in Latin America; photo (M)

2005-11-07.title: Marine Killed in Ambush on Iraq-Syria Border
2005-11-07.abstract: A marine was killed in an insurgent ambush on Sunday when his patrol raided a house in this tense border town. He was the first American casualty in a Marine-led sweep through the area aimed at stopping foreign jihadists from infiltrating Iraq through the Syrian border.     A group of marines began to search the house, which appeared to be empty, and were met with a hail of gunfire, which killed one marine and drove the others outside. The insurgent gunman was killed by other marines as he tried to flee from the roof.

2005-11-07.title: Hong Kong's Democracy Movement Seeks New Showdown
2005-11-07.abstract: Roman Catholic and Protestant leaders today called for a protest on Dec. 4 against plans to expand the security legislature.

2005-11-08.title: 16 Suspects Caught in Terror Raid Are Charged in Australia
2005-11-08.abstract: The men had chemicals similar to those in the London bombings, officials said, but no evidence has linked them to an outside group.

2005-11-08.title: Down on the Farm, by the Golf Course and Subdivisions
2005-11-08.abstract: Constant collision between two Chinas--one urban and racing into 21st century, other rural and seemingly locked in 18th, can be seen on edges of Beijing, where farmland is fast being converted into suburbs; farmers herding flocks of sheep compete for space on dusty roads with trucks carrying construction materials for latest high-rise buildings; other farmers ride carts behind tractors or even mules as sleek cars with tinted windows glide by; photo (M)

2005-11-08.title: Chile Arrests Peru Ex-Leader; Lima Is Seeking His Extradition
2005-11-08.abstract: Alberto Fujimori, disgraced fugitive former president of Peru, is arrested hours after arriving in Santiago, Chile, on first leg of what he hoped would be triumphant political comeback; Peruvian government immediately seeks his extradition to stand trial on charges involving 21 violations of human rights and acts of corruption during his decade in power; he has lived in Japan since fleeing Peru (M)

2005-11-08.title: French Officials Try to Ease Fear as Crisis Swells
2005-11-08.abstract: French government seeks to reassure jittery nation as civil unrest spreads to nearly every major city and claims its first death; Prime Min Dominique de Villepin calls up 1,500 police reserves to reinforce 8,000 police officers already waging nightly battles in streets and says some towns would impose curfews to keep marauding youths off streets; rules out for now sending army into suburbs where unrest has grown among African immigrants; government seeks to avoid aggressive tactics for fear of sparking incident that could further escalate violence; France has been slow to react to spreading violence set off by accidential deaths of two youths on Oct 27, in part because initial nights of unrest did not seem particularly unusual in country where more than 80 cars day are set on fire, even before violence; government appeared paralyzed as violence spiraled into worst civil unrest country has faced in 40 years; Pres Jacques Chirac did not speak publicly about matter until 11th day of evolving crisis; Europe is watching unfolding crisis with alarm, fearing copycat attacks; photos; map (M)

2005-11-09.title: UN renews mandate for U.S.-led Iraq force
Ambush of Defense Lawyers in Hussein Trial Kills One
2005-11-09.abstract: Gunmen ambush defense lawyers in Saddam Hussein trial, killing Adel Muhammad al-Zubaidi and wounding Thamir Mahmoud al-Khuzaie; two represented co-defendants Taha Yassin Ramadan and Hussein's half-brother Barzan al-Tikriti; two of 13 defense lawyers have now been killed and others seem determined to shun court; photo; Iraqi court officials say lawyers rebuffed 24-hour protection that judges and prosecutors have (M)

2005-11-09.title: Merkel makes gains in coalition negotiations
Provision of Blair's Antiterror Plan Is Rejected
2005-11-09.abstract: A bill allowing police to detain terror suspects for 90 days without charge was defeated, but a proposal for 28 days was approved.

2005-11-09.title: Letter from China: The cross-pollination of India and China

2005-11-10.title: U.S. offers new bid on democracy
Casinos threaten Hong Kong's largest taxpayer
House of Commons Rebuffs Blair on Antiterror Provision
2005-11-10.abstract: British House of Commons rejects crucial provision of government's antiterrorism bill, handing Prime Min Tony Blair his first defeat in Commons since coming to power eight years ago; Blair had staked much on vote, making passionate last-minute pleas to wavering legislators; government's proposal would have increased time terror suspects can be held without charge from current 14 days to 90 days (M)

2005-11-10.title: Bad Habits Linger at the Polls in Egypt
2005-11-10.abstract: Egyptians vote in first of three rounds to select 444 members of Parliament; even though government appears to keep its promises at many polling places to ensure fair vote, poll watchers elsewhere talk openly about practice of candidates paying for votes; even best of intentions cannot transform culture of Egyptian politics, where for years bribery and intimidation have been part of process; photos (M)

2005-11-11.title: Blush if You Must, for Art's Sake, but Don't Panic
2005-11-11.abstract: Museo degli Argenti in Florence, Italy, mounts major exhibition on mythology and erotica, prompting Dr Graziella Magherini, top psychiatrist in city, to urge caution; she claims viewing nude bodies can be dangerous to one's mental health; photo (M)

2005-11-11.title: A Political Rival of Mubarak Loses His Seat in Parliament
2005-11-11.abstract: Two months ago, Ayman Nour came in second to Hosni Mubarak in Egypt's first contested presidential election.

2005-11-11.title: A government on the move to a half-built capital
World Briefing | Africa: Health Agency Says Polio Is Halted In 10 Countries
2005-11-11.abstract: World Health Organization reports that polio has again been eliminated from 10 western and central African countries, but persists in northern Nigeria as rejection of vaccination by Nigerian government continues; disease remains endemic in six other countries (S)

2005-11-11.title: Gunman Attack Oman's Embassy in Baghdad, Killing 2
2005-11-11.abstract: BAGHDAD, Iraq, Nov. 11 - Gunmen sprayed Oman's embassy here tonight, killing an Iraqi guard and a Sudanese worker and injuring four others, officials said.

2005-11-12.title: Vatican Document Said to Ban Gay Priests
2005-11-12.abstract: A Vatican document would ban new priests who ''present deeply rooted homosexual tendencies'' but would not exclude candidates who suppressed any gay tendencies roughly four years before their final ordination, an Italian newspaper, Il Giornale, reported Friday.     The article presented the most detailed account yet, including direct quotations, of a document that has been in the works for a decade and has provoked heated debate on homosexuality in Catholic seminaries and in the priesthood itself.

2005-11-12.title: Jordan Seizes 12 Suspects in the Hotel Bombings
2005-11-12.abstract: The Jordanian authorities on Friday arrested the first suspects in connection with Wednesday's multiple suicide bombings, as the last of the victims were buried at cemeteries in and around the capital.      Deputy Prime Minister Marwan Muasher said security men had arrested 12 people, several of them Jordanians, in connection with the bombings, in which 60 people, including 3 bombers, have died.

2005-11-12.title: World Briefing: Americas, Africa, Asia, Europe, Middle East
World Briefing | Asia: Pakistan: Protest Over Tent Camp Move
2005-11-12.abstract: The police in Muzaffarabad, the flattened capital of Pakistani-controlled Kashmir, wielded batons against a crowd of 200 earthquake survivors protesting an order to leave their makeshift camp because it lacked drinking water and adequate sanitation, Pakistani officials said. Several people were injured, and 10 were arrested. The police say they want to move the residents of the crowded 350-tent camp into two new and bigger ones in the city with better conditions. ''We will use force, if we have to, to shift these people tomorrow,'' a police official said.   Salman Masood (NYT)

2005-11-12.title: World Briefing | Africa: Sudan: U.S. Opens Southern Consulate
2005-11-12.abstract: The United States opened a consulate in Juba, the primitive, hardscrabble town that is the capital of Southern Sudan. Robert B. Zoellick, the deputy secretary of state, officiated. The United States helped broker the peace agreement this year that ended a 21-year civil war between the mainly Muslim north and the Christian and animist south. It calls for power sharing, and Mr. Zoellick has been pushing the Sudanese government to implement its terms. Officials said the new consulate was intended to demonstrate that the United States will remain engaged. A senior official said two diplomats had so far applied to be consul general in Juba, where there is no indoor plumbing.   Joel Brinkley (NYT)

2005-11-13.title: India Arrests Suspected Leader of New Delhi Bomb Blasts
2005-11-13.abstract: Indian police asserted that the suspect has links to a Pakistan-based militant group called Lashkar-e-Taiba.

2005-11-13.title: Jordan says three Iraqis linked to al-Zaraqwi carried out Amman bombing
French Civil Unrest Subsides
2005-11-13.abstract: The recent unrest roiling France continued to subside on Sunday without having touched the capital's tourist districts, as had been feared.

2005-11-13.title: Police tighten security at Eiffel Tower, Champs-Elysees
Official Says Iraq Reconstruction Projects Are Moving Forward
2005-11-13.abstract: Also today, the U.S. military cast doubts on a claim posted on a Baath Party Web site that the last of Saddam Hussein's inner circle still at large had died.

2005-11-14.title: EU prohibits visits by 12 Uzbek officials
U.S. operation in Iraq kills 37 insurgents
French Unrest Subsides, but Violence Persists in Lyon
2005-11-14.abstract: Unrest roiling France continues to subside without touching Paris's tourists districts, as had been feared; most concentrated unrest comes in Lyon, second-most-populous metropolitcan region; Interior Sec Nicolas Sarkozy continues to take hard line against perpetrators of violence (M)

2005-11-14.title: Suicide car bomb in Kabul kills a NATO peacekeeper

2005-11-15.title: Clashes mar runoffs for Egyptian Parliament
Bomb Kills 3 in Karachi Near a KFC Outlet
2005-11-15.abstract: A powerful blast outside an American restaurant wounded at least 15 people in Pakistan's southern port city today.

2005-11-15.title: Deal reached on Gaza border crossings
Chirac to Ask for Extension of Crisis Rules to Combat Riots
2005-11-15.abstract: President Jacques Chirac, addressing his country for the first time since unrest broke out, said that he had asked Parliament to extend a national state of emergency to February and that he would set up a program that would provide jobs and training for 50,000 youths by 2007.     The president, stressing respect for the law and the need to recognize the diversity of French society, acknowledged that the past two weeks had been proof of a ''profound malaise'' in the country, calling it a ''crisis of identity.''

2005-11-15.title: Iraq to Investigate Alleged Abuse of 173 Detainees
2005-11-15.abstract: Officials will investigate whether a group of detainees found in an Interior Ministry building were tortured by their Iraqi captors.

2005-11-16.title: Pacific nations call for trade deal
Letter from America: What France can learn from 1960s New York
5 marines die in Iraq in assault near border
Ex-spy chiefs arrested in South Korea
Sarkozy ratchets up his challenge to Chirac

2005-11-17.title: A 1,200-Year-Old Murder Mystery in Guatemala
2005-11-17.abstract: Archeologists and forensic experts digging among ruins of ancient Maya city of Cancuen find as many as 50 skeletons in sacred pool and other places, victims of murder and dismemberment in war that destroyed city and, it seems, served as beginning of collapse of classic period of Maya civilization; precipitous decline of Mays is one of enduring mysteries of American archaeology; photo; map (M)

2005-11-17.title: Israel Moving Toward Early Elections
2005-11-17.abstract: With Palestinians planning their own parliamentary polls, peacemaking initiatives are likely to be put on hold.

2005-11-17.title: Italy arrests three Algerians suspected of terror links
World Briefing | Americas: Canada: Quebec's Separatist Party Picks New Leader
2005-11-17.abstract: Canada's Parti Quebec elects Andre Boisclair as new party leader, making him first openly gay leader of political party in Canada; he immediately declares hopes to push for independent Quebec and promises to hold third referendum for independence should he prevail in next election, to be held in 2007 or 2008 (S)

2005-11-18.title: At least 38 dead in mosque attacks near Iranian border
World Briefing | Asia: Kashmir: Quake Survivors Cross Border
2005-11-18.abstract: Pakistan and India allow two dozen Kashmiri civilians trapped in Pakistan after October earthquake to return to India by crossing militarized line that divides disputed territory (S)

2005-11-18.title: World Briefing | Middle East: Jordan: Bride's Mother Dies Of Bombing Wounds
2005-11-18.abstract: Mother of bride in wedding taking place in Amman, Jordan, hotel hit by suicide bombing dies of wounds; brings death toll to 59 people (S)

2005-11-18.title: Zimbabwe Said to Permit U.N. to Build New Homes
2005-11-18.abstract: Zimbabwe government continues to deny that its campaign in spring to demolish country's urban slums created nationwide housing crisis; but it now appears to have relented about allowing United Nations to construct 2,500 homes to aid victims of mass evictions (M)

2005-11-18.title: APEC tries to foster trade talks

2005-11-19.title: Halliburton Case Is Referred to Justice Dept., Senator Says
2005-11-19.abstract: Pentagon investigators have referred allegations of abuse in how the Halliburton Company was awarded a contract for work in Iraq to the Justice Department for possible criminal investigation, a Democratic senator who has been holding unofficial hearings on contract abuses in Iraq said yesterday in Washington.      The allegations mainly involve the Army's secret, noncompetitive awarding in 2003 of a multibillion dollar contract for oil field repairs in Iraq to Halliburton, a Texas-based company. The objections were raised publicly last year by Bunnatine H. Greenhouse, then the chief contracts monitor at the Army Corps of Engineers, the government agency that handled the contract and several others in Iraq.

2005-11-19.title: World Briefing | Middle East: West Bank: Israel Plans New Construction
2005-11-19.abstract: Israel has invited bids for the building of 13 homes in Maale Adumim, the largest Jewish settlement in the occupied West Bank, despite an obligation to freeze such construction under the ''road map'' peace plan with the Palestinians and a promise to President Bush not to expand settlements. Israel also issued tenders for building in Ariel and Adam, two other ''settlement blocs'' that Israel says it intends to keep in any peace agreement. Tenders have been issued for 315 housing units in the territories since the beginning of the year.   Steven Erlanger (NYT)

2005-11-19.title: Publisher Is Charged With Criticizing Turkey
2005-11-19.abstract: A Turkish book publisher said Friday that the government was suing it for distributing a translated book that the government charges is critical of the Turkish identity, army and state and the founder of the republic, Mustafa Kemal Ataturk.     The publisher, Fatih Tas, the owner of Aram Publishing, could face three years in jail for issuing the book, ''Spoils of War: The Human Cost of America's Arms Trade'' by John Tirman, which focuses on military sales to Turkey. It was published in the United States in 1997.

2005-11-19.title: World Briefing | Europe: Turkey: One Killed In Blast Near Istanbul
2005-11-19.abstract: A bomb hidden in a garbage container on the outskirts of Istanbul killed one person and wounded 10 others, the Anatolian News Agency reported. No one claimed responsibility for the blast, near a popular amusement park in the Beylikduzu district. The Istanbul governor, Muammer Guler, said security forces suspected a remote-control device caused the blast.   Sebnem Arsu (NYT)

2005-11-19.title: U.S. Aide Accused of Graft in Iraq Had a Shadowy Past
2005-11-19.abstract: One of them dressed all in black, Johnny Cash style, even in the summer in Iraq, where the temperature often rose above 120 degrees. The other put up huge blue-and-white signs advertising one of his companies -- GBG Logistics -- that remain standing even now along the dangerous road to the Baghdad airport, after car bombs and firefights have destroyed nearly everything else around them.     This week these two Americans, Robert J. Stein Jr. and Philip H. Bloom, were charged in federal court with working together in a bribery and kickback scandal. Mr. Stein, the one who favored the black attire, worked for the American occupation authorities as a comptroller and financial officer and is accused of receiving hundreds of thousands of dollars in bribes to steer lucrative reconstruction contracts to Mr. Bloom's companies.

2005-11-20.title: Iran Approves Bill to Bar U.N. From Visiting Nuclear Sites
2005-11-20.abstract: Iran's Parliament approved the outline of a bill that would bar United Nations inspectors if the agency referred Iran to the Security Council.

2005-11-20.title: Japanese fad: Comics that degrade Chinese and Koreans
Dispute Over Division of Authority at the U.N. Grows
2005-11-20.abstract: The dispute has prompted a warning from John R. Bolton, the American ambassador, that the U.S. may look elsewhere to settle international problems.

2005-11-20.title: Quake victims welcome US$5.8 billion in aid for Pakistan

2005-11-21.title: Cheney renews attack on prewar-data critics
In the Senate, a Chorus of Three Defies the Line
2005-11-21.abstract: Vice Pres Dick Cheney met privately last July with Republican Sens John W Warner, John McCain and Lindsey Graham to ask them to toe administration line on treatment of military prisoners, but senators would not budge from their push to regulate treatment of prisoners; meeting was early hint of feud that spilled into open as Senate Republicans openly challenged Pres Bush last week on American policy in Iraq and war on terrorism; though their views on war differ, they have much in common: each is member of Armed Services Committee, has strong maverick streak and has ties to military and to one another; relationships with Bush are respectful, though not close, and each has different political agenda, but their union has become powerful political force at time when Bush's popularity is sinking and Washington is consumed with debate over Iraq; they are firm in conviction that Congress must flex its muscles after ceding authority on military matters to executive branch; photos (M)

2005-11-21.title: Iran Parliament Votes to Close Atomic Sites to U.N. Monitors
2005-11-21.abstract: Iranian Parliament approves outline of bill that would bar UN inspectors from nuclear sites if agency referred case to Security Council for possible punitive measures; bill needs approval of Guardian Council to become law; board of governors of UN International Atomic Energy Agency is expected to review Iran's case at meeting later in week; agency has called on Iran to suspend uranium enrichment activities before meeting (M)

2005-11-21.title: Rail strike in France is new test for leaders
An American in Asia: Bush finds respite, briefly, in Ulan Bator

2005-11-22.title: Key points of the proposed constitution
Bosnians Vow to Create a Stronger Government
2005-11-22.abstract: Ten years after the end of the civil war in Bosnia and Herzegovina, its leaders agreed to press for constitutional changes.

2005-11-22.title: Women to get no niche in the Vatican's Swiss Guard
World Briefing | Americas: Mexico: Cartel Leader Captured
2005-11-22.abstract: Mexican prosecutors capture Ciudad Juarez drug cartel leader Ricardo Garcia Urquiza, accused of moving five tons of cocaine a month into United States; became head of cartel after former leader Rudolfo Carrillo Fuente was gunned down in turf war; photo (S)

2005-11-22.title: 18-year-old high school student sworn in as mayor of U.S. city

2005-11-23.title: Chinese city of 3 million without water
Iraqi Court Reaches Deal With Hussein's Lawyers
2005-11-23.abstract: The Iraqi court trying Saddam Hussein has reached an agreement with defense lawyers to end the boycott they declared after two of their number were killed.

2005-11-23.title: Korean lab roiled by egg donor disclosures
Pinochet Arrested in Chile on Tax Fraud Charges
2005-11-23.abstract: Gen. Augusto Pinochet, the former military dictator of Chile, was arrested today in Santiago, Chile, on tax fraud and passport forgery charges arising from secret bank accounts, holding millions of dollars, that he maintained under false names in the United States and elsewhere.

2005-11-24.title: Putin Defends Russia's Right to Regulate Private Groups
2005-11-24.abstract: Russia said it would review a controversial plan to control the activities of charities and other nongovernmental organizations.

2005-11-24.title: World Briefing | Europe: The Hague: Dividing Up Milosevic's Trial
2005-11-24.abstract: Judges trying Slobodan Milosevic are evidently anxious to safeguard at least one part of his war crimes trial, which continues to be bogged down by the poor health of the former Yugoslav president. They propose to split up the enormous trial and to carve out the part, nearly complete, dealing with the 1999 war in Kosovo. This could lead to an early judgment. The rest of the case, dealing with the wars in Bosnia and Croatia, would then follow. Mr. Milosevic's high blood pressure and vascular problems once again forced the trial to stop this week, and judges have called hearings to discuss the proposed division of the trial on Tuesday.    Marlise Simons (NYT)

2005-11-24.title: Russians seek to put restrictions on NGOs

2005-11-25.title: Palestinians Take Control of Key Border Crossing
2005-11-25.abstract: The opening today was seen as a step toward independence and a boost to Palestinian leader Mahmoud Abbas.

2005-11-25.title: Referral of Iran to UN put off
Kazakhstan, with its rough edges, faces election
Celebrations mark opening of Gaza border

2005-11-26.title: Names of the Dead
2005-11-26.abstract: The Department of Defense has identified 2,093 American service members who have died since the start of the Iraq war. It confirmed the death of the following American yesterday:    KNOP, Allen J., 22, Specialist, Army; Willowick, Ohio; 101st Airborne Division.

2005-11-26.title: A Revolutionary Channels His Inner Michael Moore
2005-11-26.abstract: FOR years, Massoud Dehnamaki was known widely as the feared enforcer of conservative rules that restricted freedom for women and society.      In recent years, however, he has emerged as Iran's Michael Moore, having directed a documentary on the taboo issue of prostitution and another forthcoming film on soccer as a metaphor of political struggle.

2005-11-26.title: Austria Refuses Bail to Briton Accused of Denying Holocaust
2005-11-26.abstract: An Austrian judge on Friday denied a request for bail for the British historian David Irving, who is accused of violating the country's laws against denying the Holocaust.      The ruling meant that Mr. Irving, 67, whose highly eccentric and widely rejected views of Nazi history have gained him worldwide notoriety, will remain in prison for at least four more weeks while Austrian prosecutors prepare an indictment against him.

2005-11-26.title: World Briefing | Mideast: Egypt: Crackdown On Islamists
2005-11-26.abstract: Egypt arrested 20 members of the largest Islamic opposition group, the Muslim Brotherhood, a day before the runoff in the second round of legislative elections, an Alexandria spokesman for the banned group said. The arrests were seen as an attempt to curb the group's successes in two rounds of the three-stage election. It has gained 47 seats this year, compared with 17 in 2000.   Abeer Allam (NYT)

2005-11-26.title: An Architect Cuts Corners, and Shakes Japan's Faith
2005-11-26.abstract: No buildings have collapsed, yet. No one has been hurt. But the case of the architect caught cheating on earthquake building codes has transfixed Japan.     Night after night, television news programs feature video clips of tearful condominium owners moving out of their new apartments, while construction company owners, inspectors and the architect involved in the deficient buildings blame one another for the failings. So far, seven hotels have been forced to close, including a 260-room tower that opened in August near the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

2005-11-27.title: Egyptian Vote Again Marred by Intimidation
2005-11-27.abstract: Egypt's parliamentary elections were marred by new charges of violence and government-sponsored intimidation as voters went to the polls Saturday in a runoff that held the prospect of candidates loyal to the banned Muslim Brotherhood adding to their representation, poll monitors said.     Though the streets were more subdued on Saturday than when the second round of voting began 10 days ago, when men attacked voters in Alexandria with knives and machetes, hundreds of Muslim Brotherhood supporters were reportedly arrested, and there were many allegations of voting irregularities.

2005-11-27.title: For sturgeon poachers, little to fear
Briefs: 10 die in earthquake in the south of Iran
Iran-North Korea talks may harden U.S. stance

2005-11-28.title: Raid in Bosnia finds proof of Islamist network
EU warns members on secret U.S. camps
Explosion in north China coal mine leaves scores dead

2005-11-29.title: U.S. Lacks Plan to CurbTerror Funds, Agency Says
2005-11-29.abstract: GAO report finds that Bush administration efforts to help foreign nations cut off money supply to terrorists are stymied by infighting among American agencies, leadership problems and insufficient financing; says that more than four years after Sept 11 attacks US still lacks integrated strategy; government officials say audit overstates bureaucratic issues; US has identified 26 countries considered particularly vulnerable to exploitation by terrorist financiers but report describes turf battles between State and Treasury departments over who is in charge of efforts in these countries (M)

2005-11-29.title: Briefly: China spends millions to clean water supplies
Europe freight line: Italians just say no
Issue of secret camps strains U.S.-EU relations
Sunnis Accuse Iraqi Military of Kidnappings and Slayings
2005-11-29.abstract: Mounting evidence suggests Iraqi forces are carrying out executions in Sunni areas, even as US military pushes largely Shiite Iraqi security forces into larger role fighting insurgency; hundreds of accounts of killings and abductions emerge in recent weeks, mostly from Sunni civilians who say relatives were taken away by Iraqis in uniform without explanation; some bodies have been found with signs of torture, some have vanished and others have turned up alive in prison, including those found tortured in Interior Ministry bunker; Min Bayan Jabr and other officials deny government role but many claims are substantiated by human rights group and by Sunni leaders; one community group reports documenting death or disappearance of 700 Sunni civilians in last four months; photo (M)

2005-11-30.title: Clinton sees gains in tsunami zone
Britain urged to raise retirement age
U.S. pushes UN for Myanmar inquiry
Merkel Makes First Major Speech as Germany's Chancellor
2005-11-30.abstract: Angela Merkel condemned the kidnapping of a German citizen in Iraq and vowed to make Germany "one of the three top countries in Europe in 10 years."

2005-11-30.title: Iraq Is Struck By New Wave Of Abductions
2005-11-30.abstract: Prominent German archaeologist and aid worker, Susanne Osthoff, who has worked in Iraq for years, is kidnapped and threatened with execution unless Germany withdraws support for Iraqi government; abduction is latest in new wave of kidnappings of Westerners after yearlong lull; Osthoff is kidnapped with driver, and videotape delivered to German television shows two people in blindfolds; aid group Christian Peacemaker Teams confirms seizure of four workers, Briton Norman Kember, American Tom Fox and Canadians James Loney and Harmeet Singh Sooden; Al Jazeera video shows four with captors who accuse them of being spies; photos; more than 200 foreigners have been kidnapped since US invasion; dozens have been killed and about 20 are still missing; gunmen kill senior Sunni cleric in Falluja, Sheik Hamza Abbas al-Esawwi, one of few powerful figures in Anwar who urged Sunnis to participate in political process; two US soldiers are killed by bomb north of Baghdad; new German Chancellor Angela Merkel appeals for release of Osthoff, who has lived in Iraq for many years and broken ties with family; Osthoff was threatened in Mosul for restoring Christian site; she also upset Shiites in south for trying to protect sites and artifacts from smugglers (M)
